title: What 'Crazy' Really Meant When He Called You That
dek: He didn’t call you crazy because you lost control. He called you crazy because you stopped making his comfort the center of the room. The word was a muzzle, not a diagnosis - and your body knew it before you did.
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. What 'Crazy' Really Meant When He Called You That is a place where the body and the spirit meet, and it asks for honest attention rather than control. In our experience, healthy anger is information about a boundary, a value, or a longing that has been crossed or unmet. This piece walks you through what to notice, what to try, and what to leave alone, in plain language and with real care.

They told you your anger was the whole problem. *Last updated January 7, 2026.* They were wrong. The word “crazy” landed in your chest like a stone dropped into still water. It sank, and you felt it lodge somewhere behind your ribs - a dull, constant pressure that made breathing shallow. You didn’t question it then because you were too busy apologizing, too busy trying to prove you were reasonable, measured, soft. The body remembers the moment the label stuck: the heat creeping up your neck while your hands went cold, the way your vision narrowed to a pinpoint, the metallic taste of swallowed words. You might have even believed him. You must have seemed unhinged, right? Your voice rose; your hands shook; you couldn’t stop the tears. What else could that be but madness? Here’s what no one told you: that surge of heat, that tremble, that fierce electric current running down your arms - it wasn’t insanity. It was clarity trying to fight its way through a sealed room. And the person who sealed it called you crazy so you wouldn’t notice the lock. ### What He Meant When He Said “Crazy” He meant “inconvenient.” He meant “I am not going to examine my behavior, so I will examine your reaction to it instead.” The word was never about your psychological state; it was about his need to control the frame. When a partner, a boss, a family member labels you crazy, they are performing an act of profound misdirection. They are taking the spotlight off their own actions - the belittling comment, the broken promise, the three-day silence - and shining it directly on your response. Suddenly, the conversation isn’t about what they did; it’s about how you *are*. You’re too sensitive. You’re overreacting. You’re unstable. You’re crazy. Karla McLaren, in her work on emotional vocabulary, describes anger as the “honorable sentinel” of the self. It rises to protect your boundaries, your voice, your sense of right and wrong. When someone calls you crazy in the heat of your anger, they are essentially telling you, “Your sentinel is defective. Your boundary is the real problem here.” And because we are socialized - especially if we are women - to value harmony over self-protection, we often agree to disarm our own sentinel. We swallow the rage, and with it, the label. The word “crazy” becomes an internal alarm that we silence with self-doubt. ### The Diagnosis That Was Never About You Consider the setup. He raised his voice, but when you matched his volume, you were “hysterical.” He lied by omission, and when you uncovered it and your face went tight, you were “paranoid.” He withdrew affection for days, and when you finally broke down - tears, snot, the ugly sob of accumulated neglect - you were “unhinged.” In each instance, the word functioned as a verdict delivered by a judge who was also the defendant. There was no jury of your peers, only a single authoritarian voice telling you that your perception was faulty. Harriet Lerner, in her decades of writing on anger and relationships, points out that when we are told our anger is excessive or irrational, we often double down on trying to present our case perfectly. We believe that if we can just be calm enough, articulate enough, unemotional enough, the other person will finally see our truth. But Lerner notes that this is a trap: the person who needs you to be calm to hear you has already set the terms of engagement to ensure they never have to listen. They aren’t waiting for a better argument. They are waiting for you to exhaust yourself. The word “crazy” is the most efficient way to deplete you before you even begin. ### A Counterintuitive Anchor: Your Fury Was the Sane Response Here is the claim that might feel like a betrayal of everything you were taught: your anger was not a symptom of dysfunction; it was evidence of health. A capacity for rage in the face of mistreatment is as vital as a capacity for love. When your body flooded with heat, when your heart hammered against your sternum like a fist demanding release, your nervous system was not betraying you. It was accurately reading the situation and mobilizing for survival. The person calling you crazy was attempting to gaslight your own biology into retreat. Robert Augustus Masters writes that anger “is not a problem to be solved but a flame to be honored and directed.” To label that flame as madness is to pathologize life force itself. The real “crazy” was the situation - the double bind, the emotional whiplash, the impossible expectation that you absorb injury without flinching. Think of a time you witnessed an animal being cornered. Its reaction - bared teeth, guttural sound, lunge - is not considered crazy. It’s considered natural. You were cornered. The word was meant to make you believe you had no right to bare your own teeth. But the body remembers what the mind was trained to deny. That shaking in your legs after the fight? That was the aftermath of power flooding muscles that were forbidden to strike. That was sanity trying to complete its circuit. ### The Body Remembers What the Story Erased Here is a sensory truth you can test right now. Close your eyes - no, not now, but after you read this - and bring to mind the last time someone dismissed your anger with that word. Not the argument details, just the moment the word hit. Notice where your attention goes in your body. Is the jaw locked? Is there a band of tension across your forehead? Do your shoulders rise toward your ears as if to guard your throat? Some people feel a hollowing in the stomach, a cold spot where the rage turned inward because it had nowhere safe to go. That cold spot is a record. The body holds what the mind was forbidden to conclude: *I was right. I was violated.* Sensorimotor psychotherapy, the approach developed by Pat Ogden, works directly with these bodily imprints, treating physical sensation and posture as a gateway to processing what words alone cannot reach. [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) maps the way trapped survival responses - like the punch you never threw or the scream you swallowed - stay lodged in the tissues. You weren’t crazy. You were a body mid-protest, cut off from its own completion. The label was a sedative injected into your muscles. Getting it out requires letting the body finish its interrupted sentence. ### Tracing the Rage Lineage: A Short Practice You can begin this unwinding in under five minutes. Find a quiet spot. Sit on the floor with your back against a wall if possible - something solid behind you. Close your eyes and place one hand on your lower belly, the other on your chest. Breathe normally, and simply track your inhale and exhale for ten cycles. Then, ask yourself this single question, directed not at your mind but at the hand on your belly: *Where in this body did the “crazy” stick?* Don’t analyze. Wait for an image, a color, a temperature, a shape. You might feel a knot under your ribs like a walnut shell, or a fizz of static in your forearms. Once you locate it, gently press into that area with your palm. Not hard - just enough to let the body know you’re there. Say aloud or silently: *That was not mine. That word was a lie told to keep the peace. This body knew the truth.* Sit with the sensation for two more minutes. If tears come, let them. If a growl rises in your throat, let it hum. This isn’t about catharsis for its own sake; it’s about reconnection. The practice restores a line of communication between the sentinel anger McLaren describes and the self that was taught to silence it. You are not venting. You are repatriating. ### FAQ: Three Questions You’ve Probably Asked at 2 a.m. **1. “But what if I actually *am* too reactive? What if there’s something broken in me?”** The fact that you’re asking this likely means you’ve been trained to turn the lens on yourself before you turn it on the situation. True dysregulation exists, yes, and it deserves care. But the “crazy” label is almost never applied by an abusive or dismissive person with clinical discernment. It’s applied to shut you up. A useful test: did this same person call you crazy when you were happy, generous, accommodating? Or only when you set a boundary, expressed hurt, or asked for accountability? If the word only appears in moments of your self-assertion, it’s not a diagnosis. It’s a silencing tactic. **2. “How do I know if my anger is clean anger or just damage?”** Karla McLaren distinguishes between “clean anger” that arises to protect a current boundary and “hooked anger” that carries old, unprocessed history. Clean anger feels present, grounded, and specific: you’re angry about *this* comment, *this* broken agreement, *this* violation in front of you. Hooked anger pulls in a lifetime of slights and overwhelms you with its volume. But here’s the counterintuitive part: even hooked anger is not crazy. It’s an archive demanding to be cataloged. The task is not to pathologize the volume but to sort through the files. A therapist trained in somatic work can help, and so can daily body-based check-ins that let small angers speak before they amass into a scream. **3. “He’s not a monster - he was stressed, tired, going through something. Does that make the word less harmful?”** Impact is not measured by intent. A brick thrown in exhaustion breaks a window just as surely as one thrown in malice. Many people wield “crazy” out of their own unskilled defensiveness, not calculated cruelty. But the harm lands in your body the same way. Harriet Lerner reminds us that understanding why someone harmed us doesn’t obligate us to remain within range of the bricks. You can have compassion for his stress and still name that the word was a weapon. Both truths can exist. ### The Tea That Won’t Fix It (But Might Steady Your Hands) None of what follows is a cure. You know better now than to believe a cup of tea can solve what institutionalized gaslighting and relational trauma built. But sometimes, in the aftermath, you need something to slow the tremors while you do the harder work. Kava root, prepared traditionally in Pacific Island cultures, has a way of loosening the jaw and quieting the electric hum in the limbs without numbing the mind. [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (60 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54ESK?tag=spankyspinola-20) is a mass-market echo of that tradition - nothing profound, nothing sacred, just a warm, earthy liquid that can soften the edge of hypervigilance on a night when your nervous system won’t stand down. Drink it slowly. Feel the heat travel down your throat into the cold cave of your stomach. Let it remind your body that it is allowed to unbend, even for ten minutes. This is not bypassing. This is harm reduction while you find your feet. ### What Ancient Formulas Knew About the Liver and Rage Chinese medicine has long understood what Western psychology is only now articulating: that anger gets stuck in the body, and that the body needs pathways to move it. The classic formula Xiao Yao San, often translated as “Free and Easy Wanderer,” was designed to address the stagnation that rises when frustration has no outlet - what the ancients called liver qi constraint. It’s a pattern of tightness in the ribs, a lump in the throat, a mind that spins on grievances, a monthly cycle that aches with more than cramps. [Gaia Herbs Calm A.S.A.P.](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B07H2QFMNR?tag=spankyspinola-20) offers a modern, shelf-stable version of this formula. Tiny, bitter pellets that you swallow with warm water. They are not a substitute for leaving the person who called you crazy. They are a quiet, daily gesture that says: this body is not your enemy. This stagnation is not my identity. These pills are a conversation between the old wisdom of herbs - bupleurum, dong quai, peony - and your exhausted liver meridian. They won’t erase the memory, but they might loosen its grip on your digestion, your sleep, your breathing. They might make room for the anger to complete its movement through you instead of camping in your tissue. ### After the Label, There Is a Voice The word “crazy” was never yours. It was a sticky note slapped onto your forehead by someone who needed you to stop looking at them so clearly. But the residue stays long after the note is removed. You catch yourself over-explaining to people who don’t deserve your story. You preface your needs with apologies. You monitor your own volume, your own pulse, as if you are a bomb that might detonate in polite company. This is the long tail of that single, weaponized syllable. It becomes a form of self-surveillance, a tic of shame that lives in the muscles between your shoulder blades. But the body does not forget its truth, and every time you honor a boundary, every time you let your voice find its natural register without artificially softening it, you are peeling off a layer of the lie. Over at [the Oracle Lover](https://theoraclelover.com), we’ve been tracing the quiet rebellion of naming what was never yours to carry - the anger, the grief, the unsatiated hunger for justice that no calm-down mantra can dissolve. The voice that “crazy” tried to strangle is still there, waiting in your diaphragm, ready to resonate at a frequency that rattles old walls. Robert Augustus Masters speaks of anger as a force that, when met directly and without shame, can deepen intimacy rather than destroy it. Not the performative anger of blame, but the clean, undefended anger that says, *You hurt me and that matters.* That version of anger doesn’t burn bridges; it clears the underbrush so you can finally see the path away from people who need you to be small. You are not required to be small. You were never crazy. You were simply loud in a room that demanded your silence. The body remembers the exact shape of the muzzle. But the body also remembers how to break it. That heat in your chest? It’s not a bomb. It’s a forge. Use it. ## What the research and the body both say In our experience, the most useful sources on this stay close to the body. For a careful clinical view, see the NIH on anger and the autonomic nervous system. If you want to keep going inside this journal, you can read [our herbal cabinet](/herbs), take a quiet hour with [more articles in this journal](/articles), or stay in the writing through [the assessments hub](/assessments). ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated January 7, 2026. In our work on what 'crazy' really meant when he called you that, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
